1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: extracted module-level `const PROCESSING_FEE = 5` and used it in the return literal instead of a bare `5`.
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — spec calls this "processing fee (R3)"; naming the literal makes the domain concept explicit and prepares for upcoming quote tests that all add +5 fee. Mass rose 2 → 3 but clarity trumps APP.
6	- Naming established: `PROCESSING_FEE` (module-private constant) for the fixed R3 fee component of a quote premium.
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: renamed local `base` to `basePremium` inside `runScenario`'s step.map callback so it mirrors the module-level `BASE_PREMIUM` lookup table and reads explicitly in the premium formula.
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — `base` alone is ambiguous (base of what?); `basePremium` matches the domain term used in the spec ("100 base") and ties directly to the `BASE_PREMIUM` map it is summed from. Mass unchanged at 30. Did not extract a `quoteStep` helper yet — too early with only 2 tests; will reconsider once item-modifier tests (curse, high-enchantment) land.
11	- Naming established: `basePremium` (local) for the sum of `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` across items in one quote step.
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted explaining variable `firstInsurance = basePremium * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` so the premium formula reads as a sum of three named domain components: `basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE`.
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the spec describes "first insurance" as a distinct premium component (10% of base), parallel to `PROCESSING_FEE`; the formula now mirrors that vocabulary directly. Mass +1 (one binding) but Rule 2 > APP per project rules. This sets up cleanly for upcoming additive components (curse surcharge, high-enchantment surcharge, loyalty discount) — each will become its own named line in the same shape.
16	- Naming established: `firstInsurance` (local) for the 10% first-insurance premium component on `basePremium`.
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- No refactoring (justified): Green added one row (`staff: 80`) to the existing `BASE_PREMIUM` table — purely additive data, no structural change. All names still reveal intent (`BASE_PREMIUM`, `basePremium`, `firstInsurance`, `PROCESSING_FEE` match spec vocabulary), no duplication present, formula reads as three named domain components. Extracting a `quoteStep`/`computeBasePremium` helper is still premature: per cycle-2 plan, defer until item-modifier tests (curse, high-enchantment) land — the next todo (`potion`) is yet another base-premium row, not a modifier. Touching the `?? 0` fallback now would pre-empt the dedicated CLI-error tests for unknown items further down the list.
20	
21	## After cycle 5
22	- No refactoring (justified): Green added one row (`potion: 40`) to `BASE_PREMIUM` — same purely-additive shape as cycle 4. No new structural opportunities emerged. Names still match spec vocabulary; no duplication; the three-component formula (basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE) still reads as the spec describes. Inlining the `results` binding would save 1 mass unit but hurt Rule 2 (the named `results` mirrors the output key). Extracting `computeBasePremium(items)` is still premature — the natural trigger is the 3-rune block-discount test (cycle 8 in the todo list), where pure lookup-and-sum first breaks down; refactoring now would be guessing at the helper's shape before seeing how component blocks fit. The `?? 0` fallback continues to be reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type error test later.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: extracted explaining variable `rawPremium = basePremium + firstInsurance + PROCESSING_FEE` so the line `const premium = Math.ceil(rawPremium)` reads as a discrete rounding step.
26	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — the rune test (32.5 → 33) made rounding-up a distinct domain step (spec R3: "rounded up to the nearest G"). Naming the unrounded total separates "compose the premium from its named components" from "round up to whole G"; both intents now stand out at a glance. Mass +1 (one binding) but Rule 2 > APP. Did not extract a `quoteStep`/`computeBasePremium` helper yet — per cycles 2/4/5, that's reserved for the 3-rune block-discount test where pure lookup-and-sum breaks down; the next test (2 runes, no block) is still pure summation.
27	- Naming established: `rawPremium` (local) for the unrounded sum of premium components, prior to round-up.
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- No refactoring (justified): Green made no code change — the 2-runes test auto-passed via the existing reduce+lookup (25+25=50 base, ×1.1=55, +5=60). Six candidates were considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeQuotePremium(items)` helper — natural trigger is the 3-rune block-discount test (cycle 8 per the todo list) where pure lookup-and-sum breaks down; extracting now is still guessing at the helper's shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6; (2) inline `rawPremium` — would reverse cycle 6's Rule 2 win; (3) inline `results` — already rejected in cycle 5 on Rule 2 grounds (mirrors output-key vocabulary); (4) inline the `scenario` cast — named cast on its own line is the conventional TS narrowing pattern; (5) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature — reserved for the CLI-error tests further down the list; (6) extract the reducer lambda — single call site, two short tokens, and the `?? 0` is tied to the deferred unknown-item-type CLI-error test. All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 41 with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.
31	
32	## After cycle 8
33	- Change: named the two magic numbers in the rune block-discount branch — introduced module-level `const RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE = 3` and `const RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE = 60`, and used them in the `if (type === "rune" && count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) basePremium += RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` branch.
34	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — Green introduced bare `3` and `60` for spec R2 (rune block discount); naming them ties the branch directly to spec vocabulary ("block of 3 runes" → "block price"). Mass +4 (two bindings + two constants at module scope) but Rule 2 > APP per project rules. Deferred again: extracting a `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper or a table-driven block-discount rule — the next two tests ("4 runes no block", "3 runes + 3 moonstones") will reshape the conditional from `count === 3` into "count of complete 3-blocks across alike-typed components"; refactoring now would guess at that shape. Also deferred: renaming `perItem` (short name, 6-line scope — clear enough), tightening `runScenario(input: unknown)` (reserved for CLI-error tests).
35	- Naming established: `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` (module-private constant) for the spec-R2 block size of 3 runes; `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` (module-private constant) for the price of one complete rune block.
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: moved `const perItem = BASE_PREMIUM[type] ?? 0` from the top of the for-loop into the `else` branch where it is actually used; the block branch (`count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE`) no longer computes a value it ignores.
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — `perItem` now sits next to its only consumer (`basePremium += perItem * count`), making the else-branch read as a self-contained per-item pricing step; the if-branch reads as a self-contained block-priced step. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) — the block branch no longer carries a dead binding/lookup. APP static element count is unchanged (~64) since the elements still exist in the else-branch, but the dead path in the block branch is gone. Did NOT extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper or generalize the block rule to `Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE)` arithmetic — both are still premature per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8: the natural trigger is "3 runes + 3 moonstones" (cycle 11 in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from "type === rune" to "any block-eligible type" and from `count === 3` to "complete 3-blocks of one type". Refactoring now would guess at that shape; the 7-rune test (cycle 10) still falls through the else-branch cleanly (7*25=175). Locality move is reversible if the block rule restructures the branch entirely.
40	- Naming established: none new (`perItem` retained in narrower scope).
41

content.text:
All 10 tests pass. Now let me evaluate refactoring opportunities for cycle 10 (the 7-rune test was the most recent).

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

Current names in `runScenario`:
- `scenario`, `results`, `countsByType`, `basePremium`, `firstInsurance`, `rawPremium`, `premium`, `perItem`, `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE`, `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`, `BASE_PREMIUM`

All match spec vocabulary. `runScenario` itself describes what it does: runs the scenario steps. Names still reveal intent across all 10 passing tests.

## Step 2: Current APP Mass

Looking at `runScenario` body:
- Constants: `{}` literal, `0`, `0`, `?? 0`-style 0, plus the literals already in module consts. Inside function body proper: roughly `{}`(1) + `0`(1) + `0`(1) = 3 constants
- Bindings: `scenario`, `results`, `step`, `countsByType`, `item`, `type`, `count`, `basePremium`, `perItem`, `firstInsurance`, `rawPremium`, `premium` ≈ 12
- Invocations: `scenario.steps.map`, `step.items` access, `Object.entries(countsByType)`, `BASE_PREMIUM[type]`, `Math.ceil(rawPremium)`, plus arithmetic ops (`+`, `*`, `+=`) — count meaningful invocations: ~6-8
- Conditionals: 1 (`if/else` for rune block) + 1 (`?? 0`) = 2 conditionals × 4 = 8
- Loops: `map`, `for...of` items, `for...of` Object.entries = 3 × 5 = 15
- Assignments: `countsByType[item.type] = ...`, `basePremium += ...` (×2) = 3 × 6 = 18

Rough total mass ≈ 65.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 10 green.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Cycle 10 added no code (7-rune test passed via existing else-branch summation). The 7-rune test exercises the same `BASE_PREMIUM["rune"] * count` path that the 4-rune test (cycle 9 setup) does — no new domain concept emerged. Names still match spec vocabulary.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: Two `for...of` loops live side by side but compute different things (count-by-type, then price-per-group). The bag-of-counts intermediate is the conventional way to express "alike items group for block pricing". No duplication to extract.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Could I inline `countsByType` for the no-block case? No — the block branch needs the per-type count, so `countsByType` is load-bearing. Could I drop the `?? 0` fallback? No — it's reserved for the CLI unknown-item-type test (per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9 deferrals).

## Step 4: Refactoring Candidates Considered

1. **Extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper** — STILL premature. Per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9, the natural trigger is the upcoming "3 runes + 3 moonstones" test (next in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type" and from `count === 3` to "complete 3-blocks of one type". Extracting now would guess at that shape; the 7-rune test passed via the same else-branch the 4-rune test exercised, no new structural pressure.

2. **Generalize block rule to arithmetic** (`Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE + (count % RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * BASE_PREMIUM[type]`) — would over-fit. The 7-rune test shows the block does NOT apply for count=7 (175, not 60+4×25=160). So the rule is genuinely "exactly one full block of 3 → block price; otherwise per-item". The next test (`3 runes + 3 moonstones`) will reveal whether the spec means "exactly one block" or "alike-type blocks of 3 across components". Refactoring the conditional now would lock in the wrong shape.

3. **Rename `perItem` to `perItemPrice`** — `perItem` lives in a 2-line scope inside the else-branch and is multiplied by `count` immediately; rename adds mass with no clarity win.

4. **Inline `rawPremium` / `firstInsurance` / `basePremium`** — would reverse cycles 3/6 Rule-2 wins.

5. **Tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature** — reserved for CLI-error tests further down the list.

6. **Extract the count-by-type loop into `countByType(items)`** — single call site, 3 lines, well-named local; extraction would add an invocation (mass +2) without a Rule-2 win. Defer until a second call site appears (claim-handling tests may bring one).

## Step 5: Decision

No refactoring applied this cycle. The 7-rune test exercised the existing else-branch summation path with zero code change in Green; no new structural pressure emerged. All previously-deferred refactorings (computeBasePremium helper, block-rule generalization, signature tightening) remain deferred for the same reasons — their natural triggers are still upstream tests (3-runes+3-moonstones, CLI errors).

## Step 6: Verify Tests Still Green

Tests already confirmed green above (10 passed, 25 todo).

## Step 7: Append to Architecture Notes

## After cycle 9
- Change: moved `const perItem = BASE_PREMIUM[type] ?? 0` from the top of the for-loop into the `else` branch where it is actually used; the block branch (`count === RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE`) no longer computes a value it ignores.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) — `perItem` now sits next to its only consumer (`basePremium += perItem * count`), making the else-branch read as a self-contained per-item pricing step; the if-branch reads as a self-contained block-priced step. Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) — the block branch no longer carries a dead binding/lookup. APP static element count is unchanged (~64) since the elements still exist in the else-branch, but the dead path in the block branch is gone. Did NOT extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper or generalize the block rule to `Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE)` arithmetic — both are still premature per cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8: the natural trigger is "3 runes + 3 moonstones" (cycle 11 in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from "type === rune" to "any block-eligible type" and from `count === 3` to "complete 3-blocks of one type". Refactoring now would guess at that shape; the 7-rune test (cycle 10) still falls through the else-branch cleanly (7*25=175). Locality move is reversible if the block rule restructures the branch entirely.
- Naming established: none new (`perItem` retained in narrower scope).

## After cycle 10
- No refactoring (justified): Green made no code change — the 7-runes test auto-passed via the existing else-branch summation (7 × 25 = 175 base, ×1.1 = 192.5, +5 = 197.5 → ceil 198). The test exercised the same `BASE_PREMIUM[type] * count` path that the 4-runes test already covered (cycle 9), so no new domain concept or structural pressure emerged. Six candidates considered and rejected: (1) extract `computeBasePremium(items)` / `priceForType(type, count)` helper — natural trigger is "3 runes + 3 moonstones" (next test in the todo list), where the conditional MUST generalize from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type"; extracting now would guess at the helper's shape, same reasoning as cycles 2/4/5/6/7/8/9; (2) generalize block rule to `Math.floor(count / RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE + (count % RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE) * perItem` arithmetic — the 7-rune test actually CONFIRMS the rule is "exactly one full block of 3 → block price; else per-item" (7 runes priced at 7×25, not 60+4×25=160), so block-arithmetic would over-fit and break the 4-rune and 7-rune tests; (3) rename `perItem` to `perItemPrice` — lives in a 2-line scope, multiplied by `count` immediately, rename adds mass with no Rule-2 win; (4) inline `rawPremium` / `firstInsurance` / `basePremium` — reverses cycles 3/6 Rule-2 wins; (5) tighten `runScenario(input: unknown)` signature — reserved for CLI-error tests further down the list; (6) extract the count-by-type loop into `countByType(items)` — single call site, 3 lines, well-named local; adds invocation mass without Rule-2 win, defer until a second call site appears (claim-handling tests may bring one). All names still match spec vocabulary; mass remains around 65 with no Rule-2-respecting reduction available.

content.text:
Refactor Phase Complete.

**Refactoring**: None applied this cycle (justified — see architecture-notes.md cycle 10 entry).

**Naming Evaluation**: All names still match spec vocabulary (`basePremium`, `firstInsurance`, `rawPremium`, `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE`, `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`). `runScenario` clearly describes its role.

**Mass**: ~65 (unchanged — no code change in Green).

**Tests**: All 10 passing, 25 todo.

**Why no refactoring**: The 7-rune test exercised the same else-branch summation path that the 4-rune test already covered (cycle 9), so no new structural pressure emerged. The natural trigger for extracting a `computeBasePremium` / `priceForType` helper or generalizing the block rule remains the upcoming "3 runes + 3 moonstones" test, where the conditional MUST generalize from `type === "rune"` to "any block-eligible type". Refactoring now would guess at that shape. The 7-rune test actually CONFIRMS the rule is "exactly one full block of 3 → block price; else per-item" (7 runes priced at 7×25=175, not 60+4×25=160), so block-arithmetic generalization would over-fit and break tests. Six candidates considered; all rejected with documented reasons.

Relevant files:
